one_line_takeaway: By grounding LLM vulnerability-detection agents in a clue-anchored, on-demand Code-Property-Graph evidence trace before they reason -- rather than debating or retrieving generic knowledge over incomplete context -- AEGIS (DeepSeek-V3.1, training-free) reaches 122/435 pair-wise-correct predictions on PrimeVul, the first system past 100, at ~$0.09/sample, though its Audit Agent still over-corrects true vulnerabilities in CWE classes (787/476/125) whose exploitability depends on absence-of-protection facts the trace cannot directly encode.
key_finding: At its default/best configuration AEGIS (k=2, DeepSeek-V3.1, training-free) achieves P-C=122 out of 435 pairs, the first method reported to exceed 100 P-C on PrimeVul's pair-wise benchmark -- a 27.1% relative improvement over the strongest baseline, VulTrial(trained, GPT-4o, P-C=96), and 50.6% over VulTrial(untrained, GPT-4o, P-C=81). Standard-metric numbers at k=2: Accuracy=56.78%, Precision=57.78%, Recall=50.34%, F1=53.82%, FPR=36.78%, VP-S=59 (the paper notes 59 is the best VP-S among training-free methods only; ReVD, which is fine-tuned, scores VP-S=68 with a much worse FPR of 58.39%). AEGIS reduces FPR relative to individual baselines by 21.96% (vs. VulTrial-trained, 47.13%->36.78%), 54.40% (vs. VulInstruct, 80.67%->36.78%, i.e., a 43.89-percentage-point absolute drop), and 37.01% (vs. ReVD, 58.39%->36.78%) -- these are the paper's own relative-reduction percentages, not FPR values themselves. Average inference cost at k=2 is $0.09/sample (cost rises roughly linearly with k, from $0.052 at k=1 to $0.355 at k=10, driven mainly by the Phase II Retrieved-Context stage). Ablation findings: (i) Phase II's CPG-guided expansion lifts clue-localization recall dramatically over Phase I alone -- from 16.3% to 71.3% at top-1 (a 55.0-point jump) and from 30.6% to 81.6% at top-3, converging by top-10 (52.1% vs. 85.3%). (ii) P-C is non-monotonic in k, peaking at k=2 (122) and declining to 112 (k=3), 93 (k=10), while Recall/F1/FPR rise monotonically with k -- the paper argues low-confidence clues at high k inject noise that hurts pair-wise discrimination even as raw recall improves. (iii) Removing the dialectical Red/Blue structure (keeping audit) is the most damaging ablation: P-C collapses from 122 to 57 at k=2 (a 53.3% relative loss) because unstructured single-pass verdicts get over-vetoed by the Audit Agent (Recall drops to 19.3%). Removing Meta-Auditing (keeping dialectics) causes a different failure: P-C drops to 94, but FPR surges from 36.8% to 60.9% because the single Verifier exhibits confirmation bias with no independent check. (iv) At k=2 the Audit Agent overturns 186/870 verdicts (21.4% veto rate); 105 (56.5%) are correct reversals of false positives and 81 (43.5%) incorrectly overturn true positives, a 1.30:1 correct:incorrect ratio; net P-C impact is positive (+28) except at k=1 where it is slightly negative (-1) because the evidence base is too thin. (v) Reasoning-flaw taxonomy at k=2 (621 flaw instances across 186 vetoes, 3.4/veto): the two largest categories are emergent (not among the four predefined types) -- Pattern-Matching Flaw (18.84%) and Semantic Misunderstanding Flaw (16.43%) -- followed by Over-Trust (16.43%), Speculation (14.01%), and Absence-as-Evidence Flaw (11.76%); outright Evidence Fabrication is rare (1.77%), which the authors read as evidence that grounding in a closed trace suppresses hallucinated-evidence claims specifically, even though other reasoning errors persist. (vi) Systematic weakness: incorrect vetoes concentrate on CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write), CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference), and CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read) -- all in the 2025 CWE Top 25 -- because their exploitability hinges on absence-of-protection conditions that a trace of what IS present in the code cannot directly encode, biasing the Audit Agent toward over-correcting genuinely correct 'vulnerable' calls on these CWE types.
core_contribution: AEGIS is a training-free, four-agent LLM pipeline for function-level vulnerability detection that separates 'what is suspicious' from 'whether it is exploitable' under a 'From Clue to Verdict' philosophy. (1) A Clue-Discovery Agent scans the target function in isolation under a 'Worst-Case Taint Assumption' (treating all non-local data -- parameters, globals, external-call returns -- as untrusted) and emits high-recall candidate clues, each a tuple <line, statement, rationale, confidence in [0.1,1.0]>. (2) A Context-Augmentation Agent performs clue-anchored, demand-driven slicing over a repository-level Code Property Graph (built with Joern): bidirectional PDG slicing within the target function, then iterative cross-file expansion -- internal calls expanded directly, external calls gated by an LLM decision on whether inspection is needed, followed by on-demand graph stitching via synthesized Argument->Parameter and Return->Call-site edges -- bounded by a per-variable slicing-depth limit of 10 and a global cap of 50 cross-function expansions. This produces a per-variable 'Evidence Trace' (T), a closed, citable evidence substrate. (3) A Verifier Agent runs a structured single-agent dialectical protocol (Factual Comprehension -> Red/attack argument -> Blue/defense argument -> Evidence-Weighted Adjudication), with every claim required to cite a specific trace line number. (4) An Audit Agent independently re-derives its own assessment from the raw trace (ignoring the Verifier's conclusion), cross-checks every citation in the Verifier's argument against the trace, and scans for four predefined reasoning-flaw categories (Phantom Mitigation, Speculation, Anchoring, Over-Trust), rendering Agree/Disagree/Defer and exercising veto power (V_final <- V_audit) only when it can name a specific material flaw. The paper explicitly positions this against two baseline paradigms it frames as sharing an 'ungrounded reasoning' root cause: multi-agent debate (e.g., VulTrial) that argues over isolated function slices, and retrieval-augmented approaches (e.g., VulInstruct, Vul-RAG) that inject generic, hypothesis-independent security knowledge (CWE descriptions, historical patches) rather than repository-specific data-flow evidence. AEGIS's own architectural claims (clue-anchored demand-driven CPG augmentation; single-agent dialectics + independent meta-audit; the CWE-agnostic, training-free design) are the paper's contribution; CPG-based slicing per se (LLMxCPG) and multi-agent debate per se (VulTrial, GPTLens) are cited prior work, not AEGIS's invention -- AEGIS's claimed novelty is the clue-anchored/demand-driven triggering of graph expansion plus the single-agent dialectic-then-independent-audit reasoning structure, not graph-based context or multi-agent reasoning in isolation.
threat_model: The paper's own framing: function-level vulnerability detection in C/C++ repositories across 140 CWE types (PrimeVul benchmark), where the analysis target is a single function f_target and its encompassing repository R, and the system outputs a binary verdict (Safe/Vulnerable). The core difficulty the paper frames is not an external attacker model but an evidentiary one: exploitability of a local code anomaly (e.g., an unchecked memcpy) depends on cross-function/cross-file data-flow facts (e.g., whether a tainted variable is sanitized in a caller, or a buffer length bounded by a callee) that are invisible when a function is analyzed in isolation. Phase I operationalizes this via an explicit 'Worst-Case Taint Assumption': since the Clue-Discovery Agent cannot see how f_target is invoked, it treats every non-local data source (function parameters, globals, external-call return values) as untrusted, deliberately over-approximating the attack surface for high recall, and defers false-positive resolution to later phases. The evaluation's hardest condition is the PrimeVul pair-wise setting: distinguishing a vulnerable function from its patched counterpart that shares at least 80% string similarity, explicitly designed to punish detectors relying on superficial pattern matching rather than genuine semantic/data-flow understanding.
limitations: From the paper's own Section 5.2 (Threats to Validity) and Section 4.3.2: (1) Implementation validity -- DeepSeek-V3.1 occasionally emitted malformed output containing 'illegal characters' (attributed to residual multilingual language-mixing), requiring a schema-validation retry layer; the authors state the retry rate was low and did not materially affect cost/runtime, but acknowledge the mitigation could subtly bias results toward first-attempt-passing responses. (2) Evaluation validity -- P-C is a strict, all-or-nothing pair metric that may underestimate partial detection capability; the authors defend this as an appropriate conservatism for vulnerability detection given the cost of both misses and false alarms, but it is a deliberate metric choice, not a neutral one. (3) Model selection -- all experiments use a single backbone (DeepSeek-V3.1) for budget reasons (GPT-4o would push the full k=3 evaluation from ~$110 to over $500); the authors argue the architecture is model-agnostic but explicitly flag that validating with alternative backbones (GPT-4o, Claude) remains future work, so single-backbone generalization is asserted, not demonstrated. (4) Systematic blind spot (Section 4.3.2) -- the Audit Agent over-corrects (incorrectly vetoes true positives) disproportionately on CWE-787/476/125 because these vulnerability classes require proving a negative (absence of a bounds check or NULL guard on the analyzed path), which the evidence trace -- which records what is present, not what is absent -- cannot directly substantiate; the Audit Agent tends to mislabel such correct inferences as 'Speculation.' (5) Scope -- evaluation is limited to C/C++ (PrimeVul); extension to other languages (Java, JavaScript) is stated as future work despite Joern's existing multi-language support. (6) Resource bounds (slicing depth 10, expansion cap 50) are fixed engineering choices whose sensitivity beyond the top-k sweep is not separately ablated.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (6,968 vulnerable / 228,800 benign functions, 140 CWE types, chronological train/test split)
PrimeVul pair-wise test set: 435 vulnerable-patched function pairs (870 functions), each pair sharing >=80% string similarity
quantitative_results: AEGIS (k=2, DeepSeek-V3.1, training-free): P-C=122/435, VP-S=59, Accuracy=56.78%, Precision=57.78%, Recall=50.34%, F1=53.82%, FPR=36.78%, avg cost $0.093/sample
VulTrial (GPT-4o, trained): P-C=96, VP-S=45, FPR=47.13% -- strongest baseline overall
VulTrial (GPT-4o, untrained): P-C=81, VP-S=31, FPR=52.64%
VulTrial (GPT-3.5, untrained): P-C=68, VP-S=6, FPR=23.45%
ReVD (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, trained): P-C=78, VP-S=68 (highest VP-S among all methods, incl. trained), FPR=58.39%, Recall=74.02%
VulInstruct (DeepSeek-V3, untrained): P-C=50, VP-S=21, Recall=85.68%, FPR=80.67%
LLMxCPG (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct, trained, reproduced by authors): P-C=28, VP-S=10, FPR=31.95%
GPTLens (GPT-3.5): P-C=20, FPR=94.71% (near-identical to trivial all-vulnerable baseline)
GPTLens (GPT-4o): P-C=44, FPR=61.84%
Ding et al. CoT (GPT-4o): P-C=40, FPR=16.55%; (GPT-3.5): P-C=18, Recall=7.82%
CodeBERT/CodeT5/UniXcoder (trained, fine-tuned encoders): P-C<=6

AEGIS FPR reduction vs. baselines (relative %, from paper's own framing): 21.96% vs VulTrial-trained (47.13%->36.78%); 54.40% vs VulInstruct (80.67%->36.78%, i.e. 43.89 pct-points absolute); 37.01% vs ReVD (58.39%->36.78%)
Phase I localization recall (top-k, ground-truth fix lines): 16.3% (k=1) rising to 52.1% (k=10)
Phase I+II localization recall: 71.3% (k=1), 78.9% (k=2), 81.6% (k=3), 85.3% (k=10)
Cost vs. k: $0.052 (k=1) to $0.355 (k=10), approx linear, ~6.8x increase
Ablation 'w/o Dialectical Structure' at k=2: P-C=57 (vs. 122 full), VP-S=26 (vs. 59), Recall=19.3% (vs. 50.3%)
Ablation 'w/o Meta-Auditing' at k=2: P-C=94 (vs. 122), VP-S=35 (vs. 59), Recall=69.0% (vs. 50.3%), FPR=60.9% (vs. 36.8%)
Audit Agent veto stats at k=2: 186/870 verdicts overturned (21.4%), 105 correct (56.5%) / 81 incorrect (43.5%), correct:incorrect ratio 1.30:1, net Delta P-C=+28

Reasoning-flaw distribution at k=2 (621 total flaw instances, 3.4/veto): Pattern-Matching 18.84%, Over-Trust 16.43%, Semantic Misunderstanding 16.43%, Speculation 14.01%, Absence-as-Evidence 11.76%, Scope Creep 7.25%, Anchoring 6.60%, Phantom Mitigation 4.19%, Incomplete Protection 1.93%, Evidence Fabrication 1.77%, Other 0.81%
baselines_compared: LLMxCPG (fine-tuned, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct, CPG-query generation; reproduced by authors)
Ding et al. Chain-of-Thought prompting (GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o, untrained)
GPTLens (agent-based two-agent debate, GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o, untrained)
VulTrial (agent-based mock-court debate, GPT-3.5 untrained, GPT-4o untrained, GPT-4o trained)
